cue: How do you say you set out to gain and ended up losing?

es.text: Ir por lana y volver trasquilado
es.literal: to go for wool and come back shorn
es.meaning: To set out to gain something and end up worse off.
es.example: Fue a reclamar y volvió multado: fue por lana y volvió trasquilado.
es.exampleTranslation: He went to complain and came back fined — went for wool, came back shorn.

it.text: Andare per suonare e tornare suonato
it.literal: to go to play and come back beaten
it.meaning: To set out to do something to others and have it done to you.
it.example: È andato a protestare e c'ha rimesso: è andato per suonare ed è tornato suonato.
it.exampleTranslation: He went to complain and lost out — went to give and got given.
it.regionNote: Claude's IT pairing UNCERTAIN — 'restarci' or 'fare un buco nell'acqua' may fit better. Reviewer to choose.

bridge.note: Both proverbs punish the aggressor by turning the verb back on them, and the reversal is built into the grammar. Spanish shears you: you went FOR wool (to take) and came back shorn (taken from), the sheep-thief fleeced. Italian plays on suonare — to play music, and colloquially to thrash — so you went to suonare someone and came back suonato yourself. A pun in one language, a shearing in the other, both saying the biter got bit.

interference.es_to_it: The Italian relies on the suonare/suonato pun and may not be the fixed form — reviewer to confirm. The wool image will not transfer.
interference.it_to_es: 'Ir por lana' has no musical pun; Spanish shears the would-be shearer.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. IT side flagged uncertain. The self-reversing verb is the shared mechanism even if the images differ.
